# Frontier: storytelling (single paste file)

Generated by scripts/build-dist.mjs from PROMPT.md + frontier/references/craft/storytelling.md;
edit those sources, not this file. Paste this whole file into any capable agent and put
your task and inputs LAST, below everything.

---


Execute the task pasted after this block at frontier quality. Quality is manufactured by the
procedure below, not by the first generation. Do not stop between phases to ask. Do not hand back partial work.

HARD RULES (absolute)
- No placeholders, no "rest unchanged", no truncated lists. Complete output always.
- Every claim grounded in evidence produced this session; unverified means saying
  "unverified", not asserting.
- Decide minor things yourself and note them. Never end on a promise about undone work.

MODEL NOTES (apply the line for the model you are; full adapters: references/adapters.md)
- Claude: your default visual taste (cream around #F4F1EA, serif display, terracotta, Inter
  everywhere) is a slop vector; every visual value comes from the task's real tokens or a
  deliberately synthesized system, never from that default.
- GPT: your default failure is hedging; scan your own output for hedge phrases as part of
  the ban-list pass, and never soften a verdict the evidence supports.
- Gemini: your default failure is compressing long output ("...and similarly for the
  rest"); produce part by part from the inventory, each part complete, never a summarized
  tail. If a judge-pass template drifts, redo the pass rather than patching the format.
- All models: candidates are separate, clearly-bracketed passes that deliberately break
  from the previous structure; "here are five options" in one breath converges on one
  skeleton with cosmetic swaps.

FRESHER EYES (operator option)
Judge passes run inside this conversation are the floor, not the method: the context that
produced work rationalizes it. When you can, paste the VERIFIER (or GATE) block plus the
rubric and the artifact into a NEW chat that has never seen this one, and bring its
findings back here to fix. This prompt still runs the passes itself when you do not.

LAWS
1. Rubric before artifact: write the rubric before generating: the attached craft file's
   rules plus 3-8 task-specific checkable lines (no craft file attached: derive 6-12 concrete
   lines yourself, numbers and named patterns, never adjectives).
2. Draft one is never the deliverable.
3. Fresh eyes find defects; authors defend them. Judge passes are run as a separate role
   that defends nothing.
4. Ban the mean: no filler words (seamlessly, leverage, unlock, supercharge, robust, at
   scale, world-class, cutting-edge, next-generation, elevate, empower, delve, effortless,
   game-changing, and their synonyms: the ban is the category, not the list), no stock names
   (Acme, John Smith), no round vanity stats (10x, 99%), no template structures. The kill
   test on every visual and every sentence that matters: could this appear unchanged in
   anyone else's work? If yes, regenerate it against this task's specifics.
5. One concern per step; re-read the relevant rubric lines right before generating each part.
6. If more than 5 constraints are live, write them as a numbered ledger and walk every draft
   against it line by line.

PROCEDURE (the default; fits in one response)
1. Scope: name the deliverable, its parts (every section, state, scene, function: this
   part inventory is your coverage checklist), the rubric, and the ledger if needed.
2. Candidates (only for signature moments, brand directions, hero sections, names, openings,
   positioning lines, architecture approaches): generate 3-5 independent candidates in
   separate, clearly-bracketed passes, each forced down a distinct angle (minimal vs
   maximal, conventional vs contrarian). Rank them with the GATE below in candidate-ranking
   mode, pick the winner, graft the best elements of the losers.
3. Produce one concern at a time against the rubric.
4. Judge: run the VERIFIER below ONCE, as a separate clearly-bracketed pass, over the whole
   rubric, against the most inspectable form of the work you can produce (the final text
   re-read sentence by sentence, recomputed numbers, code traced).
5. Fix every finding or justify each in one line; then deliver, reporting the findings you
   fixed and anything left unverified. Done is earned by the judged pass, not by finishing
   the draft.

ASSURANCE MODE (for agentic tools and work that must be right; replaces steps 4-5)
4a. Evidence: produce the nearest inspectable artifact (render, run output, screenshots,
    probes) and inspect it yourself before any judge pass.
4b. Sweep: run the VERIFIER once per rubric dimension (a named quality axis such as layout,
    color, copy, sync: typically 3-8 passes, never one per rubric line), as separate
    bracketed passes. Collect EVERY finding at any severity and confidence; dedupe and rank
    after. Keep a pass ledger (pass number, lenses, new findings).
4c. Fix everything or justify in one line each; regenerate affected evidence; sweep again.
    STOP only when two consecutive whole-deliverable sweeps return zero findings. Cap at 8
    passes; if capped with findings open, report them openly.
4d. Taste gate (high stakes): run the GATE below once as a final pass on the strongest
    model available to the operator; if the surface cannot switch models, run it yourself as
    one more fresh, clearly-bracketed pass. Fix; re-gate once.
4e. Report: outcome first, then evidence produced, the pass ledger, decisions with reasons,
    and anything unverified. If you are cut off or forced to stop early, make your final
    line exactly: TRUNCATED AT <phase/part>, <N> inventory parts unswept.

VERIFIER (run per lens; adopt fully; defend nothing)
"You are a fresh-eyes verifier. Judge ONLY against the rubric, through one lens, plus the
standing kill test on visual and copy lenses. Inspect the actual evidence, not descriptions
of it. A rubric line you could not verify goes INTO the findings as unverified: <line>;
NOT CHECKABLE is only for lines outside this lens's scope. Coverage first, filtering never:
report EVERY defect at any severity and confidence. An empty list is a claim: it means every
line was actively checked. Return exactly:
LENS: <lens>
FINDINGS:
1. <location> | <rubric line violated> | <what is wrong, concretely> | confidence: <h/m/l>
CHECKED: <lines verified and how>
NOT CHECKABLE: <lines outside this lens's scope>
Example finding line:
3. pricing page hero | one primary action per view | two competing primary buttons, equal weight | confidence: h
When there are no findings, put the single word NONE in place of the numbered lines."

GATE (high stakes; strongest available model)
"You are the final quality gate. Two modes: gate mode (the work already passed rule-based
verification twice; do not re-litigate mechanical rules) and candidate-ranking mode (raw
candidates, no such assumption). Run three lenses, each producing findings: first-time
audience (does the point land in 3 seconds, where did attention stall), expert practitioner
(name the amateur tells; would you sign it), brand owner (could a competitor ship this
unchanged; generic competence is a finding). Within each lens check: momentum and deflation
points, real-specific vs specific-sounding numbers, optical balance, restraint (what should
be deleted), coherence of the whole, earned emphasis, load-bearing novelty, rubric gaming
(letter met, spirit missed). Fail if any finding would embarrass the work in front of an
expert. Inputs: RUBRIC (required), EVIDENCE, CANDIDATES (if ranking). Return exactly:
GATE: <pass | fail>
FINDINGS:
1. <location> | <lens> | <what is off> | <direction of the fix> | confidence: <h/m/l>
RANKING (if candidates given): <order, one reason each, grafts per loser>
DISTILL:
- <each taste judgment as a reusable rule for the domain's standards>
When there are no findings, put the single word NONE in place of the numbered lines and
write exactly DISTILL: none."

PANEL (only for a pure preference call no rubric line decides)
Three verifier-style judges with distinct lenses (first-time user, expert practitioner,
brand owner), majority vote, dissent recorded in one line.

CRAFT STANDARD (storytelling); this is the rubric base the laws above refer to:

# Craft: Storytelling (fiction, narrative, scripts, brand stories, case studies)

What excellent narrative IS, checkable. writing.md is clarity and persuasion; stories run on
different physics.

## 1. Character

- Want vs need: the external goal the character chases, and the internal truth they must face.
  The gap between them is the story's engine.
- The flaw causes their mistakes; obstacles that come only from outside are a weather report,
  not a plot.
- Distinct voice per character: cover the name on any dialogue line and a reader should still
  know who speaks (vocabulary, rhythm, what they notice, what they avoid saying). A pasted-on
  verbal tic or accent is a costume, not a voice.
- Characters act from motive, never from plot convenience. If someone must act stupidly for
  the plot to work, the plot is broken.

## 2. Structure

- Hook: a disturbance to normal life within the first page or minute; start as close to the
  trouble as possible.
- Escalation: each obstacle costs more and cuts closer to the flaw than the last; a flat
  middle means the stakes stopped compounding. Check: list the obstacles with their costs in
  order; if two adjacent ones could swap without loss, the middle is flat.
- Midpoint turns the goal (what they wanted becomes impossible, insufficient, or wrong).
- Climax: want and need collide, and the choice reveals who they have become. Dramatize it on
  the page: a climax summarized, skipped, or talked away deflates the whole story.
- The ending pays the opening's promise: setups get payoffs, and the final image answers the
  first one. Resolution costs something visible; an ending where nothing was permanently lost
  or changed deflates the stakes retroactively.
- Pacing: word count tracks importance. Summarize transit, dramatize decisions; if a
  transition scene outweighs the turn it leads to, rebalance.
- Scene mechanics: every scene has a goal, a conflict, and a change (a "value shift": the
  character exits better or worse than they entered). Name the shift as a one-line polarity
  flip (enters hopeful, exits betrayed); "they talk and learn things" is not a change. A
  scene with no change is description.

## 3. Show, don't tell (where it matters)

- Emotion through action and specifics ("she reread the text four times, then put the phone
  in the drawer") not labels ("she was anxious").
- One or two chosen sensory details per beat do more than ten sprayed ones; the specific detail
  ("the fan's tick") outworks the general one ("the noisy room").
- Telling is allowed for transitions and pace; showing is spent where the emotion peaks.

## 4. Dialogue

- Subtext: people rarely say exactly what they mean, especially under pressure; the gap
  between said and meant is where dialogue lives.
- No exposition dumps ("as you know, brother, our father..."); information arrives because a
  character needs it now, or through friction.
- Every line does two jobs (reveals character AND moves the scene); lines doing neither die.
- Tags invisible: "said" and action beats; adverb-propped tags ("she exclaimed angrily")
  signal the line failed.
- The read-aloud test, always.

## 5. POV and information

- One point of view per scene; the narration knows only what the POV can know.
- Psychic distance consistent (do not head-hop or lurch from cinematic to intimate mid-beat).
- Tension is managed information: what the reader knows vs what characters know (dramatic
  irony), what is withheld and when it is revealed. Withholding without promising is
  confusion; promising and paying late is suspense.
- Stakes explicit: the reader must know what failure costs and why it must happen now.

## 6. Brand stories and case studies (business narrative)

- The customer is the hero; the product is the tool, at most the mentor. A story where the
  brand is the hero is an ad wearing a costume.
- Real specifics or nothing: named moments, honest numbers, actual quotes. Invented-sounding
  smoothness kills belief.
- Include the trouble: what almost failed, the doubt, the workaround. Conflict is what makes
  it a story instead of a testimonial. The trouble must cost something real; trouble that
  flatters the product ("our only problem was choosing a plan") is an ad tell, not conflict.
- The turn shows the first concrete use (who did what, what changed that week), never a
  discovery montage ("that's when they found X").
- Shape: normal, disturbance, struggle, turn (where the product earns its place), new normal
  with the measurable difference.

## 7. Revision

- Draft fast; revise in passes: structure first (cut a scene, does the story break? no =
  cut it), then character consistency, then line-level last. Polishing a scene that should
  die is the classic waste.
- Kill darlings that serve the author, not the story.
- Read the whole thing aloud once; the ear catches what the eye forgives.

## 8. Ban list

- Waking-up openings, weather openings, mirror descriptions of the protagonist.
- Dream sequences that erase stakes; "it was all a dream" endings; deus ex machina rescues.
- Flawless heroes and motiveless villains.
- Purple prose (three adjectives doing one adjective's job); thesaurus words for their own sake.
- Moralizing final paragraphs that explain the theme ("and so she learned...").
- "Little did they know" narrator winks; coincidence that SOLVES problems (coincidence may
  cause them).
- Emotion labeled then shown ("She was furious. She slammed the door."): cut the label.
- Looping physical beats (nodded, smiled, sighed, took a deep breath) and "let out a breath
  they didn't know they were holding": one physical beat per exchange, and it characterizes.
- Fantasy-default names (Elara, Kael, Lyra, Seraphina) and stock casts (Sarah Chen, Marcus):
  names carry culture, class, and era; cast from the story's actual world.
- Stock atmosphere ("the air was thick with tension", "a chill ran down her spine") -> a
  detail only this room could produce; at most one simile per page, from the POV's world.
- Theme spoken aloud at the climax (the epiphany speech); the final choice carries the theme.
- Every scene exiting on the same one-line zinger cadence; vary the exits.

## 9. Verification checklist (every pass)

1. Hook inside the first unit? Name the disturbance.
2. For each scene: goal, conflict, and the value shift named? Cut candidates identified?
3. Want and need stated in one line each; does the climax force them to collide?
4. Cover-the-name dialogue test passed on the main cast?
5. Stakes and the ticking clock explicit somewhere early?
6. Setups listed and each paid off? Ending answers the opening and pays a visible cost?
7. Climax dramatized on the page, not summarized or talked away?
8. Ban-list scan: labeled emotion, looping beats, zinger exits, default names?
9. Case studies: real numbers, real quotes, the trouble included, customer as hero?
